name: scroll-area
title: Scroll Area
description: A scrollable region with a styled overlay scrollbar, built on Base UI's ScrollArea. Native scrolling stays fully intact: the viewport keeps overflow: scroll, is focusable so keyboard scrolling works, and the overlay scrollbar stays visible while content overflows (never a scroll trap). The OS scrollbar is replaced by an on-token thumb (bg-border-strong, >=44px on its axis) that darkens on hover and keeps a visible border in forced-colors mode. Supports vertical, horizontal, or both axes, sizing via the viewport className, and RTL.
useWhen: Capping a long list, table, code block, or panel to a fixed height/width with a tidy custom scrollbar
Keeping a consistent, themeable scrollbar across platforms instead of the OS default
A scrolling container that must stay usable in forced-colors mode and under dir=rtl
avoidWhen: The whole page should scroll (let the document scroll natively — do not wrap the page)
Content should reflow instead of scroll at small sizes or high zoom (prefer wrapping/responsive layout)
Hiding overflow to visually clip content (use overflow utilities, not a scroll region)
Paginating large data sets (use pagination or virtualized loading)

keyboard: Tab moves focus to the scrollable viewport (it is focusable, tabIndex=0)
With the region focused, Arrow keys scroll by a small step
PageUp and PageDown scroll by a viewport page; Home and End jump to the start and end
Scrolling is never trapped — focus and the wheel/touch/keyboard all pass through natively
accessibility.targetSize: 44px project default (the thumb is floored at 44px on its scroll axis)
notes: The viewport keeps native overflow: scroll and is focusable so keyboard scrolling works; the styled overlay never replaces or disables native scroll behavior.
The overlay scrollbar stays visible while content overflows (no hover-only reveal), so it remains usable for low-vision and motor users.
The scrollbar track and thumb keep visible borders and use currentColor-independent tokens, so they remain perceivable in Windows High Contrast / forced-colors mode.
Scroll state is conveyed by thumb POSITION and LENGTH (not color alone); the thumb color only adds a hover affordance.
The region is presentational and has no accessible name of its own — give the scrolled content meaning at the call site (a heading, aria-label, or role on the content).
Color transitions are gated behind motion-reduce so they are disabled for prefers-reduced-motion users.
